claims.text: Until very recently in human history, the color blue effectively 'didn't exist' in widespread human experience because cultures had not coined a word to perceptually distinguish it from green or black.
claims.rationale: This is the central thesis of Lazarus Geiger / Guy Deutscher / Jules Davidoff's research on linguistic relativity and color perception. The framing 'blue didn't exist' is rhetorical exaggeration: ancient eyes saw the same wavelengths as ours, but they lacked a categorical label, which made it harder to distinguish and almost never named in writing. Partially true — the linguistic and historical record genuinely supports the absence of a 'blue' lexeme in many ancient languages — but the strong claim that humans literally couldn't perceive blue is overstated.

claims.text: Homer's Iliad and Odyssey are the foundational texts of ancient Greek literature and remain the headwaters from which much of modern Western literature is derivative.

evidence.quote:
homer wrote the iliad and the odyssey
and these two epic poems are considered
the foundation of all ancient greek
literature

claims.rationale: Standard literary-historical consensus. Whether Homer was a single person is debated (the 'Homeric Question'), but the foundational status of the Iliad and Odyssey for Greek literature and Western literary tradition is uncontroversial. Auerbach, Nagy, and most classicists endorse this framing.

claims.text: William Gladstone, the four-time British Prime Minister, was struck while reading the Odyssey by Homer's odd color vocabulary — calling the sea 'wine dark', honey 'green', and sheep 'violet' — and inferred Homer might have been colorblind.

evidence.quote:
he was a huge fan
of homer

gladstone was reading the odyssey and
noticed homer described the wine dark
sea not blue not green wine dark and
this description got gladstone
interested in other ways homer used
color to describe things homer describes
honey as being green and sheep as being
violet colored glad still wondered if
homer was colorblind and saw blue as
maybe something else

claims.rationale: Historically accurate. Gladstone's 1858 'Studies on Homer and the Homeric Age' devoted a chapter to Homer's color usage and proposed that the Greeks saw colors differently. He was indeed a four-time PM and an obsessive Homer scholar. The 'colorblind Homer' inference is correctly attributed to him; modern scholars (e.g., Lazarus Geiger) reframed it as linguistic rather than physiological.

claims.text: Gladstone counted color references across thousands of pages of Homer and found black mentioned ~200 times, white ~100, red 13, yellow and green fewer than 10, and blue zero — establishing a striking statistical absence.

evidence.quote:
he went through
thousands of pages of homer's writing
and counted his references to color
black is mentioned almost 200 times
white about a hundred times red is
mentioned 13 times yellow and green
fewer than 10 times but blue
zero

claims.rationale: The counts approximately match Gladstone's published tabulations. Some modern scholars dispute exact numbers (different translations and editions yield different counts), but the qualitative finding — black/white dominate, red is third, blue is essentially absent — is robust and has been re-verified by Berlin & Kay's 1969 work and later corpus linguists.

dependencies.rationale: Quantitative follow-up to Gladstone's qualitative observation

claims.text: The absence of 'blue' is not unique to Homer or Greek — researchers find it missing from the Quran, the Hebrew Bible, ancient Chinese, Hindu, and Icelandic texts, all of which use color words in roughly the same proportions Homer did.

evidence.quote:
blue
zero so gladstone looked through other
ancient greek writing nothing was ever
described as being blue gladstone's
initial thought was maybe the greeks saw
color differently than other people so
researchers analyzed ancient texts from
all over the world the quran the hebrew
bible ancient chinese hindu icelandic no
blue not once these ancient texts
reference colors with pretty much the
same proportion as homer's epics black
and white appear a lot red a few times
yellow and green appear very

claims.rationale: This generalization is well attested in linguistic anthropology — Lazarus Geiger (1860s) first showed the cross-cultural pattern, Berlin & Kay (1969) systematized it, and subsequent fieldwork on dozens of languages largely confirmed the staged emergence. Specific claim that the Quran, Hebrew Bible, etc. lack 'blue' is true in the strict sense (the Hebrew tekhelet is a dye, not a basic color term, etc.).

dependencies.rationale: Generalizes the Homer-only finding to a global pattern

claims.text: Across virtually all languages, color words emerge in the same evolutionary sequence: first black and white, then red, then either yellow or green, with blue being the very last basic color term to appear.

evidence.quote:
almost universally names for colors
emerge in the same order first a
language creates the words for black and
white the next color is always red after
red it's either yellow or green the last
color to appear in every major language
is blue and this makes sense if you
think about it

claims.rationale: This is essentially Berlin & Kay's 1969 hierarchy, which is the standard empirical result in cross-linguistic color cognition. The 'every major language' framing slightly overstates — there are exceptions and refinements (Kay & Maffi 1999) — but the basic ordering is one of the most robust findings in linguistic typology.

claims.text: Blue is rare in nature — almost no animals produce blue pigment, and most apparent blues (butterfly wings, peacock feathers, blue eyes, blue toads) are structural color from microscopic light-scattering, not pigment — so cultures had little practical need to lexicalize 'blue'.

evidence.quote:
how often does blue
appear in nature we see black and white
everywhere blood is red so humans are
familiar with that color but what in
nature is blue only 10 of flowers are
blue so pretty rare but even more rare
in animals birds mammals reptiles none
of them produce blue pigment yes there
are a couple of blue butterflies but
that's not pigment they're actually
brown the color blue is created because
of the way their wings are structured at
the microscopic level same with blue
feathers of peacocks or blue jays blue
toads even people with blue eyes that
blue isn't pigment it's the microscopic
structure of the eye or the feathers or
the skin that scatters the light in a
way that appears blue and since blue is
so rare in nature most ancient cultures
didn't see blue as a separate color but
rather a shade of green

claims.rationale: Biologically accurate. True blue pigment in animals is genuinely vanishingly rare (the obrina olivewing butterfly is one of the few exceptions); peacock, morpho butterfly, blue jay, and blue-eye coloration are all structural/scattering effects, not pigment. The '10% of flowers' figure is in the right ballpark per botanical surveys. The causal link to lexicalization is plausible and aligns with the rarity-drives-non-naming hypothesis.

dependencies.rationale: Provides the ecological mechanism for why blue is last in the lexical hierarchy

claims.text: The color blue only emerged as a distinct named category once the Egyptians invented a synthetic blue dye, which spread through the ancient world and prompted languages to evolve a new lexeme; before that, the sky was 'light black' and the Nile was 'green'.

evidence.quote:
the ocean
wasn't blue it was a shade of green the
sky wasn't blue it was light black blue
finally emerges as its own color when
the egyptians invented a way to produce
a blue dye suddenly the light black sky
was blue the nile river green for
thousands of years finally became blue
as egyptian blue dye made its way around
the ancient world languages evolved to
accommodate this new color but before
that nothing

claims.rationale: Egyptian blue (calcium copper silicate) is the world's earliest known synthetic pigment, used from c. 3100 BCE onward, and its diffusion did make blue physically and culturally salient in Mediterranean contexts. The strong claim that the dye 'caused' the lexicalization of blue is suggestive but contested — Berlin & Kay's hierarchy attributes it more to ecological-perceptual factors than to a single technological invention. The host treats one possible mechanism as the mechanism.

claims.text: Researcher Jules Davidoff's experiment with the Himba tribe of Africa — who have no word for 'blue' but many words for shades of green — showed Himba participants struggled to single out a blue square from 11 green squares but could effortlessly distinguish a slightly different green that Westerners cannot.

evidence.quote:
jules davidoff conducted an
experiment with the himba tribe in
africa and like ancient cultures the
himba have no word for blue to the himba
blue is just a shade of green so himba
participants were shown 12 squares on a
screen 11 of the squares were green one
was blue can you pick out the blue
square now of course you can you found
it immediately but the himba had trouble
with this color they had a lower
reaction time than you or i would but

this square is slightly lighter than the
others and most of us can't see any
difference if we study the colors and
try to really focus we might get it but
it's not easy but the himba they saw
this different green as easily as we
found the blue square the himba don't
just have one word for green they have
unique words and whole categories of
words for many shades of green because
of this the himba people are already
primed to recognize them

claims.rationale: The Davidoff/Roberson Himba experiments are real and were published (Roberson, Davidoff et al. 2005). However, replication has been mixed and the strong 'they couldn't see the blue' framing was later criticized — including by Davidoff himself. The host correctly notes (in claim 10) that media reports overstated this. Net: the experiment exists, the slowed-reaction effect is real, but the magnitude has often been embellished in popularization.

claims.text: The widely-circulated claim that the Himba 'literally couldn't see the blue square' is a media fabrication invented by outlets like the BBC and Business Insider — illustrating that mainstream science journalism sacrifices accuracy for narrative.

evidence.quote:
quick side note you'll see lots of
reports about this study that say the
himba couldn't see the blue square at
all that's not true the bbc and business
insider made that part up it's a
complete fabrication oh wait a minute i
thought the media follows the science
well so the media only follows science
if it fits their story uh it would seem
so

claims.rationale: The factual core is accurate: numerous popular outlets did report that the Himba 'couldn't see' blue, and this overstates Davidoff's actual findings (slower reaction time, not zero perception). Davidoff has personally complained about this misreporting. The broader editorializing about 'media follows narrative not science' is opinion. The first half of the claim is empirically well-supported; the second is host commentary.

dependencies.rationale: Corrects a common over-reading of the same Himba experiment

claims.text: Until a culture has a word for a color, that color is genuinely difficult for its members to perceive and define — the word 'pink', for example, doesn't appear in English until roughly the 13th century, and before then 'pink' was simply 'a lighter shade of red'.

evidence.quote:
the word pink doesn't appear in
english until about the 13th century
before then
pink was red a lighter shade of red

claims.rationale: The word 'pink' as a color name actually entered English much later — around the late 17th century, derived from the flower Dianthus 'pinks'. The 13th-century date appears to be wrong; the host may be confusing it with the flower-name's earlier use or with another color word like 'rose'. The broader linguistic-relativity point about color terms emerging slowly is sound, but this specific historical claim is dubious.

claims.text: Isaac Newton's seven-color spectrum (red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, violet) was an arbitrary numerological choice — Newton initially saw 11 bands, then 5, and finally settled on 7 to match the days of the week and Pythagoras's 7 musical notes, rather than from quantitative measurement.

evidence.quote:
newton himself had trouble separating
the bands he initially thought there
were 11 unique colors but then he
thought maybe just five but a week has
seven days and there are seven tonal
notes in a musical octave and pythagoras
thought seven was a magical number so
newton said effort let's call it seven
colors in modern science we measure
colors in nanometers so we could say
with precision that pure yellow is 580
nanometers but newton didn't use
wavelengths he just eyeballed it

claims.rationale: Newton's 7-color choice is widely documented as having been influenced by his Pythagorean/musical-harmony aesthetics rather than any optical necessity. He explicitly drew analogy to musical scales in the Opticks. The 11-then-5-then-7 progression is reported in several Newton biographies (e.g., Westfall). The point that this was qualitative eyeballing rather than wavelength measurement is correct — wavelengths were not measured until the 19th century.

claims.text: Normal human vision is trichromatic (three cone types — red, green, blue — yielding ~1 million distinguishable colors), colorblind people are dichromatic (two cones, ~10,000 shades), and a small number of women are tetrachromatic — possessing a fourth cone that can in principle distinguish ~100 million colors.

evidence.quote:
vision is trichromatic this means
we have three different photoreceptors
in our eyes that detect color called
cones we have cones for red green and
blue and every color we see is a
combination of these and this allows us
to see about a million different colors
and people who are colorblind only have
two functioning cone cells which lets
them only see about 10 000 shades this
is diachromatic vision and most mammals
are dichromats but a few years ago a
doctor in northern england was found to
have four types of cone cells so her
vision isn't trichromatic it's
tetrachromatic this means she doesn't
just see a million colors she can
distinguish up to a hundred million
different colors

claims.rationale: Standard color vision physiology is correct: trichromacy with red/green/blue cones, ~1 million distinguishable colors as the textbook estimate, dichromacy in colorblindness, and most non-primate mammals being dichromats. The 'doctor in northern England' refers to Dr. Gabriele Jordan's identification of Mrs. cDa29 (Dr. Concetta Antico is a separate, US case) — the 100-million-color upper bound is theoretical (4^4 / 3^4 × 1M); the actual perceptual gain is debated and may be much smaller in practice.

claims.text: Because ~8% of men are colorblind (carrying a mutant cone gene on the X chromosome), simple genetics predicts ~12% of women should be tetrachromatic — meaning millions of women may have a hidden ability to see ~100x more colors than the rest of us, but mostly don't realize it.

evidence.quote:
about one in twelve men are
colorblind so about eight percent of
them based on this statistic around 12
percent of the entire female population
should be tetrachromatic with the
ability to see a hundred million colors
a hundred times more than the rest of us
so where are all these women and what
extra 99 million colors do they see

claims.rationale: The genetic arithmetic is roughly right (Hardy-Weinberg from a recessive X-linked allele predicts ~12% of women carry the heterozygous condition). However, the leap from 'genetically tetrachromatic' to 'functionally tetrachromatic' is huge — Jordan's research suggests only a small fraction of those with the genotype actually display measurable enhanced discrimination. The '100 million colors' figure is a theoretical maximum, not an empirical observation. Reasonably popular framing, but oversimplified.

dependencies.rationale: Extends the tetrachromacy finding to a population-level prediction

claims.text: Tetrachromatic women generally don't realize they have access to extra colors — paralleling how ancient peoples were unprimed to see blue — but in lab vision tests, primed tetrachromats can rapidly and accurately distinguish color subtleties invisible to trichromatic observers.

evidence.quote:
well they don't even know they have
access to these colors just like the
ancient people weren't primed to see
blue as its own color women with
tetrachromatic vision haven't been
primed to see all these new colors so
one tetrachromatic woman was given a
vision test and she was shown a sequence
of colors in rapid succession people
with normal vision see these colors as
identical but this woman was able to see
the subtle differences her brain was
primed to make her aware that some of
the colors she was going to see could be
different and she was able to pick out
these small changes instantly and
accurately so what do these colors look
like to the rest of us well there's no
way to describe it

claims.rationale: The host is paraphrasing Jordan's lab study where one subject (cDa29) outperformed trichromats on color-discrimination tasks. That single subject's results are real and replicable in that lab. Whether the findings generalize, and whether the 'priming' explanation is correct, is more speculative. The analogy to ancient blue-perception is rhetorically pleasing but not yet demonstrated as a mechanistic parallel.

dependencies.rationale: Provides the empirical evidence behind the population-level prediction
Re-applies the linguistic-relativity thesis to modern tetrachromats

claims.text: Color is not a property of objects but a wavelength interpretation produced jointly by light, the visual system, and culture/language — meaning, in a strong sense, color does not exist outside of perception.

evidence.quote:
isaac newton learned that
colors are not part of material objects
color is just the way light is absorbed
reflected and scattered by a surface and
if you think of it that way color
doesn't really exist it's just an
interpretation of a wavelength bolado a
neuroscientist at university college
london said there's such a thing as
light there's such a thing as energy
there's no such thing as color color is
nothing more than the product of light
our culture our language

claims.rationale: This is the orthodox modern view in neuroscience and philosophy of perception (color as a 'response-dependent' or 'dispositional' property). 'Bolado' is likely Beau Lotto, a well-known UCL neuroscientist who has popularized exactly this framing. The host correctly conveys mainstream visual neuroscience consensus, though the 'color doesn't exist' phrasing is rhetorically aggressive — there are realist counter-positions in philosophy of color (e.g., physicalist or dispositionalist accounts that grant color objective status).

dependencies.rationale: Provides the theoretical underpinning for why language-shaped perception of color is possible at all
